coredata.dc:title: The intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms regulated by functional carbon nanodots for the phytoremediation of multi-metal pollution in soils
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Xiufeng
Chen, Qiong
Xu, Liang
Zhao, Rui
Li, Tao
Ci, Lijie
coredata.dc:description: Functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) were currently demonstrated to regulate plant behavior in the agricultural and environmental areas. However, their regulation mechanisms on the interactions of plant-soil system during phytoremediation remain unrevealed. Here, Solanum nigrum L. was employed to explore the intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms regulated by FCNs in the phytoremediation of Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils. The mediation of FCNs on metal removal and plant growth showed a hormesis manner, wherein the maximum induction effect was contributed by 15 mg kg-1 FCNs. Cd/Pb removal were enhanced by 8.5% and 31.6%, respectively. Moreover, FCNs reallocate metal distribution in plant by immobilized metals in roots and suppressed metal translocation to leaves. Improving plant growth (by 82.8% for root), stimulating plant hormesis, and activating plant detoxification pathways are the intrinsic mechanism for the phytoremediation smartly regulated by FCNs. Notably, FCNs induced soil enzyme activities that associated with soil nutrients recycling, up-regulated the microbial diversity and the soil immune system, and regulated S. nigrum L. to recruit beneficial microbials in the rhizosphere. The above-mentioned comprehensive improvement of soil micro-environment is the extrinsic mechanism regulated by FCNs. This study provides new insights to evaluate the interactions of nanomaterials with plant-soil system under soil contamination.

head.ce:title: The intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms regulated by functional carbon nanodots for the phytoremediation of multi-metal pollution in soils

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) were currently demonstrated to regulate plant behavior in the agricultural and environmental areas. However, their regulation mechanisms on the interactions of plant-soil system during phytoremediation remain unrevealed. Here, Solanum nigrum L. was employed to explore the intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms regulated by FCNs in the phytoremediation of Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils. The mediation of FCNs on metal removal and plant growth showed a hormesis manner, wherein the maximum induction effect was contributed by 15 mg kg-1 FCNs. Cd/Pb removal were enhanced by 8.5% and 31.6%, respectively. Moreover, FCNs reallocate metal distribution in plant by immobilized metals in roots and suppressed metal translocation to leaves. Improving plant growth (by 82.8% for root), stimulating plant hormesis, and activating plant detoxification pathways are the intrinsic mechanism for the phytoremediation smartly regulated by FCNs. Notably, FCNs induced soil enzyme activities that associated with soil nutrients recycling, up-regulated the microbial diversity and the soil immune system, and regulated S. nigrum L. to recruit beneficial microbials in the rhizosphere. The above-mentioned comprehensive improvement of soil micro-environment is the extrinsic mechanism regulated by FCNs. This study provides new insights to evaluate the interactions of nanomaterials with plant-soil system under soil contamination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Soil heavy metal contamination is a major global environmental problem resulting from rapid industrialization and human activities, such as industrial waste discharge and sewage irrigation [6,52]. Cd and Pb, commonly discharged through similar industrial processes, can severely impact soil quality and biota, including animals, plants and microorganisms [37,5,7,8]. Moreover, their persistence and bioaccumulation properties make them potential threats to human health through the food chain [48,59] due to their properties of persistence and bioaccumulation. Therefore, it is crucial to reduce associated risks and maintain environmental and ecological health [2] through the decontamination of heavy metal pollution. Being cost-effective and environmental-friendly, phytoremediation has been extensively studied and utilized in the field for addressing heavy metal contamination. However, its application is often hindered by long processing times, soil fertility limitations, pollutant bioavailability, and phytotoxicity issues [52,71]. Consequently, efficient strategies are required to enhance the phytoremediation of heavy metals in soil. In this regard, the emerging field of nanotechnology offers new possibilities for the remediation of contaminated soils [22,52].

Thanks to the unique structure and performance advantages of nanomaterials [18,50,57], the use of nanomaterials to enhance phytoremediation has emerged as a promising approach for the decontamination of polluted soils [22]. Previous studies have shown that Fe-based and carbon-based nanomaterials, such as nanoscale zero-valent iron (nZVI), nanobiochar, and carbon nanotubes (CNTs), are commonly employed to assist in the phytoremediation of heavy metals. They are demonstrated to be capable of directly removing metals, promoting plant growth, altering metals phytoavailability and alleviating abiotic stress for the beneficial of phytoremediation [33,52]. For instance, multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) at 500 mg kg-1 have been observed to improve plant growth, enhance the accumulation of heavy metals, and alleviate metal-induced toxicity in Solanum nigrum L. Chen et al. [17] However, some studies have reported contradictory results, indicating that certain nanomaterials can have negative effects on plant growth and development [42,50]. The root and shoot length of rice plant was decreased by CNTs at 500 mg kg-1 [26], while maize growth was significantly inhibited by CNTs at 100 mg kg-1 [39]. The responses of plants to CNTs vary depending on the material concentration and plant species [16]. Numerous studies suggest that higher concentrations of nanomaterials generally have a greater potential for phytotoxicity [50,59]. Thus, there is an urgent need to explore novel carbon-based nanomaterials that offer high efficiency and lower environmental risks to further enhance the phytoremediation of heavy metals.

Currently, the water-soluble functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) synthesized through a green electrolysis method have shown the ability to increase plant growth throughout the entire biological cycle, alleviate drought stress, and maintain enzyme function in the arid land at 3 mg kg-1 [13]. In saline-alkali soils, FCNs at a concentration of 8 mg kg-1 were found to promote tomato growth and productivity [11]. Given these findings, FCNs hold great potential as an alternative for assisting in phytoremediation. Hence, we hypothesized that FCNs could have positive effects on the phytoremediation of Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils. S. nigrum L., a well-known Cd hyperaccumulator, exhibits strong tolerance to heavy metal stress [37]. Additionally, it has the ability to accumulate Pb, Cd and As with the assistance of microbial agents [38,54], and remediate multi-metal contaminated soils through the use of amendments and bioaugmentation [5]. Consequently, S. nigrum L. was selected in the present study to investigate the potential intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms regulated by FCNs in the phytoremediation of Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils. This study aims to explore the dose-dependent effects of FCNs on the phytoremediation of Cd and Pb in S. nigrum L., as well as the changes in plant growth and biochemical indicators influenced by FCNs. Furthermore, the effects of FCNs on soil properties, soil enzymes activity, soil microbial structure and metabolism, and metal behavior after remediation will be highlighted in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Preparation and characterization of FCNs
ce:section.ce:para: FCNs used in this study were synthesized by an electrochemistry method as described previously [15]. Briefly, two anodes (graphite, 99.9%, Alfa Aesar Co. Ltd.) were inserted into deionized water to allow for electrolysis by a direct current power (15 V) for 120 h. The electrolyzed solution was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm. After removing the precipitate, the supernatant was freeze-dried for further use.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-1011, Japan), Malvin laser particle size analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Britain), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker Tensor 37, Germany), Raman spectrometer (invia, Renishaw, Britain), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, America) were used to analyze the morphology and structure of FCNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Soils and plants
ce:section.ce:para: Soils were collected from the surface layer (0–20 cm) of a farmland located in northern China. After removing the residues and stones, the soils were air dried and sieved through a 2 mm mesh. The main physicochemical properties of the selected soils are provided in Table S1. To create simulated metal-polluted soils, the soils were spiked with Cd (2 mg kg-1) and Pb (200 mg kg-1). Cd(NO3)2·4 H2O and Pb(NO3)2 were dissolved in ultrapure water and thoroughly mixed into the soils. The chosen concentrations of heavy metals were based on the current soil pollution status in China [69] and the Risk Control Standard for Soil Contamination of Agricultural Land in China (GB15618–2018, 5.5 <pH≤6.5, 0.3 mg kg-1 for Cd and 90 mg kg-1 for Pb).

S. nigrum L. seeds were surface sterilized with 2% (v/v) hydrogen peroxide solution, and thoroughly rinsed with sterile water. The sterilized seeds were sown in pots filled with vermiculite and half-strength Hoagland’s nutrient solution. The seedlings were allowed to grow for 2 weeks under constant temperature (25 oC), with 16/8 h light/dark cycle and 75% relative humidity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental design
ce:section.ce:para: The seedlings were transplanted into the polluted soils, with or without the addition of FCNs for the evaluation of remediation efficiency. The experiment included six different treatments. CT is the negative control without any pollution. CPS is the control of single phytoremediation by S. nigrum L. for Cd-Pb co-contamination without the addition of FCNs. CPSF1, CPSF2, CPSF3, and CPSF4 are the four treatments involved FCNs-supported remediation for Cd-Pb co-contamination. FCNs were added to the soils at concentrations of 5, 10, 15, and 20 mg kg-1, respectively. The details of the experimental design are listed in Table S2. Two seedlings were transplanted into one pot. Seedlings in each pot were cultivated for 60 days under the same conditions as described earlier. All experiments were conducted in triplicate (three pots). At the end of the experiment, plants were harvested, rinsed with flowing water, and separated into three parts (roots, stems, and leaves) for subsequent analysis. Soil samples were also collected and either air-dried or refrigerated, depending on the specific purpose of the determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of metals in soils and plants
ce:section.ce:para: A modified three-stage procedure, recommended by the European Community Bureau of Reference (BCR), was employed for the sequential extraction of metals in the soils [49]. Different fractions of metals were then analyzed using a flame atomic absorption spectrometer (AA-6880 F, Shimadzu, Japan).

Plant samples were first oven-dried at 105 oC for 30 min and then further dried at 70 oC until constant weight was achieved. The Cd and Pb content in plant roots, stems and leaves were obtained by digesting them with a HNO3/HClO4 (3:1, v/v) solution, as described previously [8,29]. The Cd and Pb concentrations were determined using an inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS, Agilent 7900, USA). To evaluate the translocation efficiency of S. nigrum L. with or without FCNs, the translocation factor (TF), and bioaccumulation factor (BAF) were calculated using the following equations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of plant growth and biochemical parameters
ce:section.ce:para: The lengths of the roots and shoots were measured to assess plant growth. The fresh weight (FW) of the roots, stems and leaves was determined immediately after harvesting. Malondialdehyde (MDA) in fresh leaves was reacted with thiobarbituric acid at 95 ◦C for 25 min, and quantified at 532 nm by subtracting the non-specific absorbance from plant tissue [27]. The levels of reduced glutathione (GSH) and oxidized glutathione (GSSG) in the plant leaves were determined using 2-vinylpiridine method and measured colorimetrically at 412 nm [1].

Fresh leaves were used to determine the activity of antioxidant enzymes. Superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity was determined using the method of nitroblue tetrazolium reduction in xanthine and xanthine oxidase reaction systems, and recorded at 560 nm [41]. Catalase (CAT) activity was assayed at 240 nm by measuring the consumption rate of H2O2 [3]. POD activity was at 470 nm (Zhang et al., 1995). Peroxidase (POD) activity was measured by the guaiacol oxidation method and expressed as the increase in absorbance at 470 nm [9]. Ascorbate peroxidase (APX) activity was defined as the decrease rate at the absorbance of 290 nm during the oxidation of L-ascorbic acid [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of soil enzymes and microbial community
ce:section.ce:para: Dehydrogenase (DHO) activity in the soil was measured using a colorimetric method at 492 nm, based on the production of triphenyl formazan (TPF) [31]. The absorbance of 578 nm was recorded to estimate soil urease (UA) activity [32]. To analyze soil microbial community structure, high throughput sequencing technology was employed and conducted at Shanghai Personalbio Technology Co., Ltd. (China), following previously described methods [5]. In brief, the V3-V4 regions of the 16 S rRNA gene were selected for bacteria amplification using the primer set 338 F-806R. For fungi, the ITS1 genes were amplified using the primer set ITS5-ITS2. Illumina MiSeq platform (USA) and QIIME2 (v.2019.4) were applied for the sequencing and bioinformatics analysis. To predict microbial community functions, the PICRUSt (phylogenetic investigation of communities by reconstruction of unobserved states) tool was utilized [56]. Then KEGG (Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes) pathway and MetaCyc (Metabolic Pathways From all Domains of Life) were used for function classification of bacteria and fungi.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: SPSS 19.0 (IBM, Armonk, USA) software were applied for the statistical analyses. Data were presented as mean values of three independent replicates with standard deviation (SD). After testing for normality and homogeneity of variances, significant differences (p < 0.05) between treatments with or without FCNs were analyzed by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Origin 2021 and Adobe Illustrator 2020 were used to produce and arrange all figures in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results
Morphology and functional groups of FCNs
ce:section.ce:para: The FCNs utilized in this study are nanodots characterized by a narrow particle size distribution (2–6 nm), according to the morphology picture of TEM and particle analyzer (Fig. S1a). The Raman spectrum exhibited distinct D and G peaks (Fig. S1b), which are attributed to material defects. The intensity ratio of these peaks (ID/IG, 0.86) suggests the presence of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of FCNs, contributing to the observed defects [13]. Carbon and oxygen are the main elements of FCNs by XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S1c). Furthermore, FT-IR and C1s XPS spectra (Fig. S1d, e) indicated the presence of major functional groups in FCNs, including carboxyl and carbonyl groups (1716 cm-1, 1573 cm-1, 1456 cm-1), hydroxyl groups (3080 cm-1, 1358 cm-1) and ester groups (1245 cm-1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs promoted plant growth under metal contamination
ce:section.ce:para: The root length, shoot height, and biomass in each part of the hyperaccumulator S. nigrum L. under heavy metal co-contamination with or without FCNs are presented in Fig. 1. As expected, plant growth was significantly (p < 0.05) inhibited by Cd-Pb co-contamination compared to the control, particularly in terms of root development. Fortunately, the addition of FCNs mitigated this inhibitory effect. The growth of S. nigrum L. gradually improved with increasing concentrations of FCNs in the soil, although a decline in growth was observed at higher concentrations (20 mg kg-1). According to the biomass changes for roots, stems and leaves, the co-contamination led to a substantial decrease in fresh shoot biomass, while no significant changes were observed in root biomass compared to the control (p < 0.05, Fig. 1). Furthermore, the presence of FCNs at concentrations of 10 and 15 mg kg-1 significantly increased plant biomass under co-contamination when compared to that without FCNs (p < 0.05, Fig. 1).

Interestingly, the effects of FCNs on the growth of S. nigrum L. exhibited an inverted U-shaped dose-response pattern. The maximum induction of shoot height was observed at a concentration of 10 mg kg-1, resulting in a growth increase of 46.9% (Fig. 1). Similarly, the maximum induction of root length occurred at a concentration of 15 mg kg-1, leading to a growth increase of 56.5% (Fig. 1). In terms of plant biomass, the maximum induction effect for shoot biomass was observed at 15 mg kg-1, resulting in a growth increase of 40.8%. For root biomass, the maximum induction effect was also observed at 15 mg kg-1, with a significant (p < 0.05) growth increase of 82.8% (Fig. 1). These findings indicate that FCNs at concentrations of 10 and 15 mg kg-1 exerted the most pronounced growth-inducing effects on S. nigrum L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of FCNs on the accumulation and translocation of Cd-Pb in S. nigrum L
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the impact of FCNs on the uptake, accumulation and translocation of Cd-Pb in S. nigrum L., the metal concentrations in the roots, stems, and leaves were determined after phytoremediation ( Table 1). In the co-contaminated soil without FCNs, the concentration of Cd was higher in the stems (9.5 mg kg-1) and leaves (7.0 mg kg-1) compared to the roots (1.4 mg kg-1). However, with the addition of FCNs at all concentrations (5, 10, 15, 20 mg kg-1), the Cd concentration in the roots significantly (p < 0.05) increased compared to the control (CPS). The highest Cd concentration in the roots (3.0 mg kg-1) was found in the CPSF3 treatment. In contrast, the presence of FCNs resulted in a reduction of Cd concentration in the shoots, particularly in the leaves, where the Cd level dropped dramatically. When 20 mg kg-1 of FCNs was added to the phytoremediation system, Cd was not detected in the leaves of S. nigrum L.

Similarly, Pb concentration in the roots (8.1 mg kg-1) was substantially lower than that in the stems (125.0 mg kg-1) and leaves (101.4 mg kg-1) in the control (no FCNs, Table 1). The application of FCNs significantly (p < 0.05) increased the Pb concentration in the roots, with higher concentrations of FCNs resulting in elevated Pb levels. The highest Pb concentration in the roots of S. nigrum L. was observed in the treatments with 20 mg kg-1 FCNs. The variation in Pb concentration in the stems was relatively mild, with only FCNs at concentrations higher than 15 mg kg-1 inducing a significant decrease in Pb accumulation compared to the control. However, the presence of FCNs led to a sharp reduction in Pb concentration in the leaves, even at the lowest application (5 mg kg-1). Notably, the effect of FCNs on the phytoremediation of Pb was much stronger than their effect on Cd.

TF and BAF were calculated to further evaluate the impact of FCNs on the translocation and accumulation of Cd-Pb in S. nigrum L. (Table 1). Overall, the application of FCNs at all concentrations resulted in a significant decrease in TF for both Cd and Pb. Although there was a slight fluctuation in the treatment of CPSF2, higher concentrations of FCNs generally led to lower TF values for Cd and Pb. Specifically, the TF for Cd declined from 6.1 (without FCNs) to 0.9 (with FCNs at 20 mg kg-1), while the TF for Pb decreased from 14.0 (without FCNs) to 0.1 (with FCNs at 20 mg kg-1). Consistently, the effects of FCNs on the BAF of metals by S. nigrum L. showed similar trends, with a significant inhibition observed in the accumulation of Cd and Pb in the shoots. The presence of FCNs resulted in a reduced bioaccumulation of Cd and Pb in the above-ground parts of the plant.

To account for biomass variations, the amounts of Cd and Pb accumulated in each part of the plants after phytoremediation of Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils are presented in Table S3. The overall trends observed are similar to the concentration behavior, wherein the presence of FCNs increased the accumulation of Cd and Pb in the roots while decreasing their contents in the leaves. However, there is a difference in the case of stems. Although the concentrations of Cd and Pb in the stems were inhibited by FCNs (5, 10, 15 mg kg-1), the overall amounts of these metals in the stems were higher than the control due to the increased biomass of the plant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal removal and speciation in soils influenced by FCNs
ce:section.ce:para: The total removal of Cd and Pb from the soils was determined to assess the influence of FCNs on the phytoremediation efficiency of S. nigrum L. ( Fig. 2). The dose-response relationship between FCNs and Cd removal rates exhibited an inverted U-shaped pattern. Cd removal rates increased with lower FCNs additions but were depressed at higher concentrations (20 mg kg-1) (Fig. 2a). Notably, the addition of FCNs at 15 mg kg-1 resulted in the highest Cd removal, with an increase of 8.5% compared to the control. Similar trends were observed for Pb removal, wherein the addition of FCNs (15 mg kg-1) led to a significant increase in Pb removal by 31.6% (Fig. 2c). These results suggest that FCNs can enhance the removal of both Cd and Pb from the soils during phytoremediation.

Different chemical forms of Cd and Pb were analyzed to assess their bioavailability and potential safety after phytoremediation (Fig. 2b,d). The results revealed that the Oxides Fe/Mn fraction of Cd increased proportionally with lower FCNs additions, indicating the transformation of Cd into less bioavailable forms. However, the residual fraction of Cd was elevated in the treatments with higher FCNs concentrations (15 and 20 mg kg-1). For Pb speciation, no exchangeable fraction was detected in any of the treatments, indicating a low mobility of Pb in the soil. Notably, addition of FCNs led to a decline in the residual fraction of Pb, while the Oxides Fe/Mn fraction of Pb was uplifted by the presence of FCNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs mediated oxidative stress and plant detoxification
ce:section.ce:para: The MDA contents in S. nigrum L. were measured to evaluate the effects of FCNs on oxidative damage in plants under Cd-Pb stress ( Fig. 3a). Cd-Pb pollution can lead to lipid peroxidation in the tissues of S. nigrum L, causing oxidative damage. Fortunately, the application of FCNs (5 and 10 mg kg-1) significantly (p < 0.05) reduced the MDA contents, indicating a reduction in lipid peroxidation. Interestingly, no significant (p < 0.05) changes in the level of lipid peroxidation were detected with the application of 15 mg kg-1 of FCNs. Unexpectedly, 20 mg kg-1 FCNs led to a dramatic rise in MDA contents, suggesting a potential exacerbation of oxidative damage.

The responses of antioxidant enzyme activities and nonenzymatic antioxidants were examined (Fig. 3b,c,d). Both SOD and APX activities exhibited a similar pattern, increasing at lower concentrations of FCNs but depressing at higher concentrations. The peak activity for SOD was observed with the addition of 15 mg kg-1 FCNs, while for APX, it was induced by 10 mg kg-1 FCNs. In contrast, POD activity significantly (p < 0.05) declined at 5 mg kg-1, while increased at 15 and 20 mg kg-1 FCNs. Conversely, CAT activity showed an opposite trend to that of POD. Despite the enzyme activities, the antioxidants such as GSSG and GSH were also mediated by FCNs. The addition of 5 and 10 mg kg-1 FCNs resulted in increased GSH contents compared to the control without FCNs. However, GSH contents were depressed by FCNs at 15 and 20 mg kg-1. The response of GSSG contents followed a similar trend to that of GSH. It is worth noting that the ratio of GSH/GSSG (0.09) in S. nigrum L. exposed to 15 mg kg-1 FCNs was the highest among all treatments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs changed soil nutrients and soil enzymes
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in soil physicochemical properties after phytoremediation with the addition of FCNs are presented in Table S1. The cation exchange capacity (CEC) of the soil was significantly (p < 0.05) raised by the addition of FCNs at 5 and 10 mg kg-1, while it decreased at 20 mg kg-1. Overall, the content of soil organic matter (SOM), available nitrogen (AN), available phosphorus (AP), and available potassium (AK) decreased with the addition of FCNs compared to the single phytoremediation treatments. The DHO activity in the soil treated with FCNs (10, 15, 20 mg kg-1) after phytoremediation with S. nigrum L. was significantly higher than in the control (p < 0.05, Fig. 4). Moreover, the addition of FCNs at 15 mg kg-1 resulted in a significant (p < 0.05) rise in soil UA activity, while other concentrations of FCNs showed insignificant impact on UA activity (Fig. 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs improved the micro-environment in polluted soils
Diversity and structure of soil bacteria and fungi
ce:section.ce:para: By high-throughput pyrosequencing, approximately 18,720 and 1420 OTUs for the bacterial and fungal community were yielded respectively in all treatments after phytoremediation ( Table 2). The Good’s coverage of bacterial and fungal sequences exceeded 98%, indicating that the sequencing depth accurately represented the libraries. The Chao1 and observed species metrics revealed that the bacterial diversity in the treatments with 10 mg kg-1 FCNs was significantly (p < 0.05) lower than in the control. However, no significant (p < 0.05) differences were observed in the Shannon and Simpson indices of bacterial community across all treatments. Interestingly, The Chao1 and Observed species of the fungal community was significantly increased by the addition of FCNs (15 mg kg-1), while these indices were decreased at 20 mg kg-1. Similarly, the Shannon and Simpson indices were both significantly (p < 0.05) reduced by the addition of 20 mg kg-1 FCNs. These results suggest that FCNs can influence the diversity and structure of both bacterial and fungal communities in the soil. And the effects on fungal diversity were concentration-dependent, with an optimal effect observed at 15 mg kg-1 FCNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Taxonomic composition and structure of soil bacteria and fungi
ce:section.ce:para: The relative abundance of bacterial and fungal community at phylum and genus level was assayed to evaluate the changes of microbial structure in the phytoremediation system facilitated by FCNs ( Fig. 5). After phytoremediation of Cd-Pb co-contaminated soil by S. nigrum L., the bacterial community was dominated by Actinobacteria and Proteobacteria, followed by Chloroflexi, Bacteroidetes, Patescibacteria, Gemmatimonadetes, and Acidobacteria. Upon the addition of FCNs at 15 mg kg-1, we observed higher relative abundances of Chloroflexi and Gemmatimonadetes, while the relative abundances of Proteobacteria and Patescibacteria decreased. Nevertheless, an increased abundance of Patescibacteria and a decreased abundance of Acidobacteria were observed at 20 mg kg-1 FCNs. At the genus level (Fig. S2), Actinophytocola, Kribbella, Actinomadura, Devosia, and BIrii41 exhibited dramatic decreases, while Nocardioides increased in the polluted soil with the addition of FCNs (15 mg kg-1). Conversely, the abundance of Actinophytocola and Pseudonocardia in the FCNs treatments (20 mg kg-1) was higher than in the control. The heatmap of the top 50 soil bacterial genera (Fig. 5) revealed that FCNs at 15 mg kg-1 clustered tightly with the control, indicating their similarity in the bacterial community structure.

Concerning the soil fungal structure, the most abundant phyla were Ascomycota, Olpidiomycota, and Basidiomycota (Fig. 5). It is worth noting that FCNs addition dramatically increased the abundance of Ascomycota, while decreased the abundance of Olpidiomycota. Additionally, other phyla such as Glomeromycota and Mortierellomycota showed slightly higher abundances under FCNs (15 mg kg-1) treatments compared to the control. In contrast, Rozellomycota was more abundant in the FCNs treatments at 20 mg kg-1 compared to the control. At the genus level, Myceliophthora and Mycothermus exhibited a noticeable increase in abundance with the addition of 20 mg kg-1 FCNs (Fig. S3). The heatmap analysis of the top 50 fungal genera (Fig. 5) revealed that the treatments with 15 mg kg-1 FCNs and the control were clustered close to each other, similar to the bacterial community.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional gene prediction of soil microbial community
ce:section.ce:para: KEGG and MetaCyc functional classification were employed to predict the active genetic functions in the microbial community [56,58] influenced by FCNs application. To be noted, the soil fungal functional profiles were not remarkably shifted by the addition of FCNs in the phytoremediation system (Fig. S4, Fig. S5). However, bacterial functions showed changes in response to the presence of FCNs. Interestingly, the immune system pathway appeared in the treatments with FCNs, which was not observed in the control (Fig. S6, Fig. S7). No other variations in metabolic pathways were observed in the bacterial community.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion
FCNs smartly regulated phytoremediation through reallocating Cd/Pb in plants - promoting phytoremediation while protecting plant safety
ce:section.ce:para: Phytoremediation has emerged as a promising method for cleaning contaminated soils [5]. The efficiency of phytoremediation for heavy metals is affected by various factors, including soil characteristics, types and concentrations of metals, and the extraction efficiency of plants. In our study, S. nigrum L., known as a Cd hyperaccumulator, exhibited excellent ability for Cd accumulation in the Cd-Pb co-contaminated system, as evidenced by a TF of 6.1 and BCF of 10.0 (Table 1). Interestingly, the uptake and translocation of Pb in the co-contaminated system is also notable, with a TF value of 14.0, surpassing the threshold of 1 (Table 1). However, Pb is mainly accumulated in roots (with TF lower than 1) in the single Pb system, or the combined pollution of Cd, Pb, Hg, and Zn [70]. The observed variation in the behavior of S. nigrum L. for Pb accumulation indicated that the presence of Cd in the plant roots enhances the mobility of Pb from roots to shoots, resulting into the elevated translocation of Pb by S. nigrum L. in Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils. This finding further highlights the potential of S. nigrum L. as a promising hyperaccumulator of Cd-Pb in the complex co-contaminated sites.

Nanomaterials have shown promise in promoting phytoremediation to heavy metals [16,23]. In the present study, the effect of FCNs on the phytoremediation of Cd-Pb was found to be dose-dependent. The lower concentration of FCNs (5 mg kg-1) did not significantly (p < 0.05) influence the removal rate of Cd/Pb. Nevertheless, the concentration or amount of Cd/Pb in the roots increased significantly, while it decreased significantly in the leaves compared to the control (Table 1, Table S3). At medium concentrations (10–15 mg kg-1), FCNs significantly (p < 0.05) increased the removal rate of Cd/Pb by S. nigrum L. except for Cd removal with the addition of 10 mg kg-1 FCNs (Fig. 2). The amounts of Cd/Pb in the leaves were significantly (p < 0.05) reduced, while their enrichment in the roots and stems increased (Table S3). This redistribution of Cd/Pb within the plant resulted in an overall increase in the removal rate of these metals. However, at a higher concentration (20 mg kg-1) of FCNs, the enrichment of Cd/Pb in the roots further increased, while their enrichment in the stems and leaves decreased (Table S3). In fact, no heavy metals were detected in the leaves, indicating a sharp decrease in the phytoremediation efficiency. This suggests that the high concentration of FCNs may lead to an excessive immobilization of Cd/Pb in the roots, hindering their translocation and subsequent removal from the system.

In fact, regardless of the different removal rates observed, the performance of FCNs at the four concentrations (5, 10, 15, 20 mg kg-1) remained consistent in terms of increasing root enrichment and reducing leaf enrichment to ensure plant safety. This is further supported by the suppression of TF and BAF with the application of FCNs compared to the control (Table 1). These findings indicate that FCNs have the ability to mobilize metals in roots while preventing their transport into leaves, thereby protecting plants from metal-induced damage. Furthermore, this protective function appears to be more pronounced with higher concentrations of FCNs.

As previously noted [22], although some nanomaterials can promote the uptake of pollutants by plants, they also carry the risk of amplifying the phytotoxicity of these pollutants. Therefore, striking a balance between increased metal uptake and the potential phytotoxicity is crucial. Interestingly, our study reveals that FCNs at 15 mg kg-1 not only promote phytoremediation but also intelligently reallocate the distribution of heavy metals within plants, making the remediation process safer while ensuring its effectiveness. This approach allows for long-term and sustainable remediation, while the increase in biomass also contributes to carbon fixation. Therefore, 15 mg kg-1 FCNs can be considered as an optimal concentration for promoting phytoremediation by S. nigrum L. in Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils. With higher FCNs concentrations (>20 mg kg-1), the protective effect becomes stronger, leading to undetectable levels of metals in plant leaves. This indicates that higher FCNs concentrations may be more suitable for safe crop cultivation in metal-polluted soils, as they provide enhanced protection against metal toxicity. Therefore, from the perspective of protecting crop safety, higher FCNs concentrations (>20 mg kg-1) are more appropriate.

The enhanced phytoremediation by FCNs can be attributed to several possible mechanisms, which are discussed in the following four aspects. Firstly, the abundant functional groups of FCNs, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl (Fig. S1), provide favorable conditions for metal binding. This capability helps alleviate the toxicity of metals to plants. The reduced metal stress subsequently improves plant growth [5,12], leading to higher biomass (Fig. 1), which in turn promotes phytoremediation. Secondly, FCNs have been shown to promote root development. This leads to the formation of a large root system, increasing the contact area between roots and metals in the soil. Consequently, the adsorption of metals onto the root surface is enhanced, facilitating their removal from the soil. Thirdly, similar to other nanomaterials [7,23], FCNs may act as carriers for Cd/Pb. Metals absorbed onto FCNs can easily enter root cells by penetrating the cell walls. Nanomaterials have been found to trigger cell wall loosening, produce new pores, or enlarge existing pores in the cell wall [22,35,45]. This phenomenon promotes the uptake of metals into S. nigrum L. Lastly, FCNs may serve as a sequestration pool for metals [4] within the soluble fraction of root cells. By sequestering metals, FCNs inhibit their transportation between different plant organs. This mechanism helps reduce the translocation of metals to above-ground parts, thereby minimizing potential risks associated with metal accumulation in edible plant tissues.

Metal mobility and its toxicity to plants grown in soils mainly depend on metal speciation [5,68]. In the present study, the application of FCNs led to an increase in the residual fraction of Cd in the remediated soil (Fig. 2), indicating a reduction in the toxicity of Cd to soil biota. The enhanced removal rate and reduced mobility of Cd further support the success of phytoremediation with FCNs. Regarding Pb speciation, no exchangeable fraction was detected in any of the treatments, which is mainly attributed to the strong absorption of Pb to soil organic matters and FCNs. The lower mobility of Pb resulted in a dramatically lower removal rate compared to Cd (Fig. 2). Despite the lower removal rate, the toxicity of Pb in the remediated soil was substantially suppressed. Importantly, the presence of FCNs led to an increase in the Oxides Fe/Mn fraction of Pb, indicating the transformation of Pb into more stable forms bound to iron and manganese oxides [68]. This suggests that FCNs can facilitate the conversion of Pb into less bioavailable and more chemically stable forms. These findings highlight the ability of FCNs to alter the chemical forms of Cd and Pb during phytoremediation, reducing their bioavailability and potentially enhancing the safety of the remaining metals in the soil.

As discussed above, FCNs prove to be excellent regulators for phytoremediation by S. nigrum L. in Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils. They can effectively reallocate the distribution of metals in plant tissues, protect plant safety, improve removal rate, and reduce metal mobility in the remaining soils. In summary, the application of FCNs during phytoremediation offers a comprehensive approach to address Cd and Pb contamination. It not only promotes the removal of metals from soil but also modifies their chemical forms, reducing their bioavailability and potential toxicity. FCNs serve as valuable regulators in enhancing the efficiency and safety of phytoremediation strategies. Further research is needed to elucidate the underlying mechanisms involved and to assess the long-term effects of FCNs on soil health and ecosystem sustainability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The intrinsic mechanisms of phytoremediation regulated by FCNs
FCNs promote plant growth to dilute metal toxicity
ce:section.ce:para: Plant biomass plays a crucial role in determining the efficiency of phytoremediation. In this study, the different responses of root length and root biomass for S. nigrum L. indicated that although root elongation was depressed, S. nigrum L. employed an alternative strategy to cope with pollution by increasing root reproduction. This phenomenon reflects the plant's resistance mechanism, known as the "dilution effect," where higher root biomass helps to relatively reduce the pollution level and prevent plant injury. This response further confirms the ability of S. nigrum L. as a hyperaccumulator. The stimulation of plant growth (82.8%) observed with the addition of FCNs (15 mg kg-1) in this study surpasses the growth promotion reported for other nanomaterials such as MWCNTs, where plant biomass increased by 37.1% at 500 mg kg-1 [17]. The growth promotion of S. nigrum L. by FCNs should be partly due to the increase of roots vigor [12], resulting in improved water uses efficiency and enhanced absorption of essential nutrients [13]. What’s more, FCNs may serve as carriers for nutrients transportation and water delivery, as observed with other carbon nanomaterials [55]. Besides, the potential utilization of carbon nanomaterials (e.g. nanobiochar) as nanofertilizers [10] in agriculture could also contribute to the growth enhancement of S. nigrum L. The substantial improvement in plant growth achieved with FCNs, even at lower concentrations, suggests their potential as an efficient material for enhancing phytoremediation.

Plant hormesis is an important intrinsic mechanism for plants to cope with external stressors [20]. It is a beneficial mechanism that allows plants to overcome adverse conditions and improve their resilience. In this study, the effects of FCNs on S. nigrum L. growth exhibited an inverted U-shaped dose-response pattern, which is a typical phenomenon of plant hormesis [6,20]. The stimulation of plant growth observed with the addition of FCNs at appropriate concentrations aligns with the concept of plant hormesis. This result indicated that FCNs have the ability to stimulate plant hormesis, leading to improved growth and performance, which is beneficial for phytoremediation in Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils. By promoting adaptive responses and enhancing plant growth, FCNs can contribute to the overall effectiveness of phytoremediation strategies. This hormetic effect may help plants mitigate the adverse effects of heavy metal contamination and improve their ability to remove contaminants from the soil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs activate plant detoxification pathways
ce:section.ce:para: FCNs could also impact the antioxidant systems responsible for plant detoxification. As an indicator of lipid peroxidation in plants, the accumulation of MDA is primarily caused by reactive oxygen species (ROS) [40], which reflects the level of oxidative stress in plants. In the present study, the MDA content in plants treated with 20 mg kg-1 FCNs was dramatically higher than in the control (Fig. 3), suggested that the plants experienced severe oxidative damage. Similar findings of aggravated oxidative damage have been reported in other plants interacted with nanomaterials. For instance, elevated MDA levels were observed in ramie exposed to Cd polluted soil with S-nZVI [23] and in S. nigrum L. under Cd-As stress with MWCNTs [17]. Since the BAF of Cd and Pb were significantly (p < 0.05) decreased (Table 1) in this study, the increased oxidative stress should not ascribe to the uptake of metals that aggravate toxicity to plants. Instead, it is likely due to the accumulation of FCNs on the root surface. At higher concentrations, FCNs are prone to agglomerate, forming aggregates that adhere to the root surface. These aggregates can restrict root respiration [24], thereby inhibiting plant growth (Fig. 1). In addition, they might also block the channels responsible for metal transportation, which is consistent with the decline in the metal removal rate (Fig. 2).

Interestingly, our results showed that the accumulation of MDA was reduced by lower concentrations of FCNs (5, 10, 15 mg kg-1, Fig. 4), indicating the alleviation of oxidative stress in plants under Cd-Pb co-contamination by FCNs. The maintenance of ROS homeostasis is attributed to the well-equipped plant detoxification system [40], including enzymes such as SOD, CAT, POD, APX, and GSH [43,44,47]. These enzymes could be activated by FCNs. When excessive ROS are produced, SOD is in charge of converting highly toxic ROS (O2•−) into less toxic ROS (H2O2). Subsequently, three detoxification pathways are activated to scavenge H2O2. The first pathway involves CAT and POD, which converts H2O2 into water. The second pathway is the ascorbate-glutathione cycle, where H2O2 is scavenged by APX [40]. In addition, H2O2 could be reduced into water when GSH is transformed into GSSG. Therefore, the ratio of GSH/GSSG reflects the redox status of cells. In the present study, the application of FCNs at 15 mg kg-1 induced the highest GSH/GSSG ratio and promoted the activities of SOD and POD (Fig. 3). These findings indicate that the antioxidant defense system was activated by 15 mg kg-1 FCNs, wherein glutathione peroxidase cycle and POD were the main contributor to scavenge H2O2. As a result, FCNs successfully defended against the oxidative stress generated by Cd-Pb co-contamination by activating plant detoxification pathways.

This activation of plant detoxification pathways plays a crucial role in improving the efficiency of phytoremediation by reducing oxidative damage and enhancing the overall performance of plants in Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils. Our findings demonstrate that FCNs activate plant detoxification pathways, leading to the alleviation of oxidative stress in plants exposed to Cd-Pb co-contamination. The activation of antioxidant defense systems, including the SOD, POD, and GSH/GSSG ratio, indicates the successful defense against oxidative damage. These results highlight the importance of FCNs in regulating the plant detoxification system, which contributes to the enhancement of phytoremediation efficiency in Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The extrinsic mechanisms of phytoremediation regulated by FCNs
Soil quality and nutrient cycling beneficial for plant growth
ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution not only inhibits plant growth but also influences the soil environment, including physicochemical properties, enzyme activities, and microbial communities [5]. These factors are often used to evaluate soil quality [66]. Hyperaccumulators like S. nigrum L. have the ability to combat heavy metal stress by recruiting potential beneficial microorganisms in the rhizosphere zone [70]. The improved soil environment, in turn, positively affect the efficiency of phytoremediation. In the present study, the application of FCNs altered the interactions between plants, soils, and microorganisms, suggesting that FCNs could mediate the soil micro-environment during the phytoremediation of Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils.

At lower concentrations of FCNs, soil CEC was significantly raised (Table S1). This can be mainly due to the presence of abundant functional groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl, on the surface of FCNs. However, at a concentration of 20 mg kg-1, the CEC decreased due to the aggregation of FCNs and their adherence to the root surface, as evidenced by the increased MDA contents (Fig. 3). FCNs could serve as carriers to transport nutrients such as N, P and K into plants [11]. Consequently, the increased plant biomass resulting from FCN application leads to greater nutrient consumption, which in turn causes a decrease in available N, P, and K in the soil. Another possible reason for the decline in bioavailable nutrients is that nanomaterials often have a binding affinity for soil nutrient elements [61].

Soil enzyme activities are crucial for the cycling of nutrients in the soil [51]. For example, DHO activity is closely associated with the bio-hydrolysis of organic compounds. The increased DHO activity induced by FCNs can accelerate the decomposition of soil SOM, which caused the descending in SOM contents (Table S1). Soil UA activity is responsible for the transformation of organic N. Therefore, the increased UA activity observed with the application of FCNs (15 mg kg-1, Fig. 4) is beneficial for the improvement of soil fertility and plant growth.

In summary, the application of FCNs can influence soil properties, including CEC, nutrient availability, and enzyme activities. At lower concentrations, FCNs can increase the CEC and promote nutrient uptake by plants, resulting in improved plant growth. However, at higher concentrations, FCN aggregation and adherence to the root surface can lead to decreased nutrient availability and enzyme activities, potentially impacting soil fertility. Understanding these extrinsic mechanisms of phytoremediation regulated by FCNs is essential for optimizing their application in Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulation of microbial structure and metabolism - the improved micro-environment
ce:section.ce:para: Soil enzyme activity is closely related to the structure and function of the soil microbial community [30], both of which are highly sensitive to changes in the soil environment [67]. The Shannon index represents the richness and evenness of the microbial community, while the Chao 1 index provides information on species richness [34]. In the present study, the Chao 1 and Shannon indices of the bacterial community were not significantly (p < 0.05) different from the control (Table 2), indicating that FCNs have a limited impact on the diversity of the bacterial community in the phytoremediation of Cd-Pb co-contamination soils. Notably, the diversity of the fungal community was significantly (p < 0.05) increased by the application of FCNs (15 mg kg-1), suggested that FCNs may enhance plant growth and improve phytoremediation efficiency by promoting the recruitment of beneficial fungi in the rhizosphere. What’s more, the up-regulation of microbial diversity is also indicative of improved soil productivity [62].

Although the diversity of the soil bacterial community was not substantially changed, the taxonomic composition showed fluctuations with the application of FCNs (Fig. 5). Actinobacteria is reported to be associated with the organic matter decomposition, carbon cycle, and N cycling [60]. Because of its generally tolerance to exogenous stressors, such as drought and heavy metals [5,13], they are the most abundant phylum in all treatments of Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils. Chloroflexi, on the other hand, play a critical role in photosynthesis in soils [21,26] and thrive in nutrient-limited environments [65]. Gemmatimonadetes is another oligotrophic phylum related to soil carbon cycling [46]. The increased relative abundances of Chloroflexi and Gemmatimonadetes observed with the application of FCNs (15 mg kg-1) are in line with the higher DHO activity and the lower availability of nutritional elements. Moreover, the increased abundance of the phylum Patescibacteria aligns with the higher levels of MDA in the FCNs treatments (20 mg kg-1), because Patescibacteria was thought to be correlated with plant stress under soil pollution conditions [5]. The elevated abundance of Nocardioides, capable of degrading recalcitrant pollutants [22], with the application of FCNs at 15 mg kg-1 further supports the notion that FCNs could shift the composition of the soil microbial community to enhance its tolerance. This speculation is also supported by the appearance of immune system pathways in the treatments with FCNs (Table S7), which has been suppressed by Cd-Pb co-contamination.

Regarding the structure of the fungal community, the abundance of Ascomycota was found to be improved with the application of FCNs (Fig. 5). Ascomycota have been reported to be efficient in cellulose decomposition and synthesis of soil humic-like substances [53]. They could also utilize many resources in the soil such as sugars and polysaccharides, thus promoting plant resistance to stress [63]. Therefore, the promotion of Ascomycota abundance by FCNs likely contributed to the growth of S. nigrum L. in metal-contaminated soils. In addition, the increased abundance of Glomeromycota is noteworthy. Glomeromycota could form arbuscular mycorrhizal (AM) symbiosis with most terrestrial plants, enhancing nutrient acquisition by the host plants [36], and providing protection against diverse biotic and abiotic stresses [19,64]. The higher abundance of Glomeromycota observed with FCN application further supports the notion that FCNs can promote plant growth and enhance metal resistance. Furthermore, Mortierellomycota, known for their ability to solubilize P [25], also exhibited higher abundance with FCN application. This finding provides additional evidence for the ability of FCNs to promote plant growth and metal resistance. The enhanced availability of P through the action of Mortierellomycota can contribute to improved nutrient acquisition by plants, further supporting their growth and metal tolerance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, the optimal concentration of FCNs for enhancing phytoremediation by S. nigrum L. in Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils was found to be 15 mg kg-1. The uptake of Cd and Pb by the roots was significantly elevated, while the translocation of metals from the roots to the shoots was dramatically suppressed by FCNs. This smart regulation by FCNs led to a redistribution of Cd and Pb within the plant tissues, making the remediation plants safer while ensuring the remediation effect, thereby achieving long-term and sustainable remediation. Combined with the reduced mobility of the remaining metals in the soil, FCNs could substantially reduce the potential risk of Cd-Pb co-contamination to soil biota.

The mechanisms by which FCNs mediate phytoremediation can be summarized in two aspects. For the intrinsic mechanism, FCNs could improve plant growth, stimulate plant hormesis, and activate plant detoxification pathways to defend against exogenous stress. For the extrinsic mechanism, FCNs could mediate the soil micro-environment in the phytoremediation of Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils. On one hand, FCNs induce soil enzyme activities associated with soil nutrient recycling, leading to improved soil fertility and plant growth. On the other hand, FCNs promote microbial diversity and activate the soil immune system to buffer against environmental variations. Moreover, FCNs could boost plant growth and resistance through regulating S. nigrum L. to recruit potentially beneficial microorganisms in the rhizosphere. Consequently, the present study provides new insights into the interactions between FCNs and the plant-soil system in the phytoremediation of hazardous materials. Furthermore, it has significant implications for the exploration of sustainable soil remediation strategies.

Further research is needed to delve into the underlying mechanisms and clarify the genetic basis of FCNs regulation. Additionally, before FCNs can be applied in field-scale remediation, several important aspects need to be addressed. The influence of environmental factors, such as pH and temperature, on the phytoremediation efficiency regulated by FCNs should be explored in future studies. Different soil types and metal species should also be taken into consideration to assess the generalizability of FCNs across varied conditions. Furthermore, the safety of nanomaterials is a crucial concern prior to their real-world application. Encouragingly, our team conducted a biological toxicity evaluation experiment using Zebrafish as a model organism, which demonstrated that FCNs were relatively harmless (with a half lethal concentration > 1000 mg L-1) [14]. This finding provides a promising prerequisite for the safe field application of FCNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Environmental implication
ce:section.ce:para: Soil heavy metal contamination is a major environmental problem worldwide. Currently, exploration of novel nanomaterials with high efficiency and lower environmental risk is urgent for better facilitating the phytoremediation of heavy metals. Functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) was just proved to be “Relatively Harmless” using zebrafish model by our team. The present study confirmed our hypothesis that FCNs could successfully regulate the intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms in the phytoremediation of Cd-Pb co-contaminated soils by Solanum nigrum L. It will provide new insights to evaluate the interactions of nanomaterials with plant-soil system in the phytoremediation of hazardous materials.
